description: flood. Location: intense rain fall at night, river floods city, many killed in bed; >2000 people, many animals; wall destroyed; also farms in valley of Edessa and Haran. Source: Pseudo-Dionysius (ed. Siegfried and Gelzer) 70; Chronicle of Edessa. (202 AD)

name: Santa Marinella, Italy
description: flooding. Location: (earlier) force (uiolentia, presumably flooding) of a river (probably the Castelsecco) and sea destroyed a bridge which was now rebuilt.. Source: L'année épigraphique 1973, no. 226. (205 AD)

name: Palestine
description: no rain. Location: Rabbi prays for rain at first in vain. Source: Bavli Ta'anit 25a, tr. Sperber 1974, 275. (210 AD)

name: Africa
description: Location: rains ruined harvest previous year (i.e. 211). Source: Tertullian, Ad Scapulam 3.2. (211 AD)

name: Capua
description: previous flooding had cut off road. Location: (previous) flooding had inundated a road at Capua, Caracalla restores it. Source: CIL 10.6876. (212 AD)

name: Egypt
description: Nile flood normal?. Location: flood normal? 6-7 m Alexandria. Source: Bonneau 1971, 254. (213 AD)

name: Tiber Rome
description: flood. Location: Terrible thunder and lightning storm, Colosseum struck, Tiber rises suddenly and floods Forum and nearby streets, sweeping people away: omen. Source: Dio Cassius, Epitome 79, 25, 2-5. (217 AD)

name: Africa
description: rains and age destroy road some time before rebuilding. Location: road destroyed by rains and age now rebuilt with bridges. Between Cirta and Rusicade, in place called "Smendu". Source: ILS 5869. (239 AD)

name: Germany, Pannonia
description: Danube and Rhine normally freeze in winter. Location: Rhine and Danube normally freeze so hard in winter horses and wagons can travel on them, and axes are needed to draw water from them. Source: Herodian, History 7.6.6-7. (240 AD)

name: Africa
description: roads destroyed by torrents sometime earlier. Location: Emperor Philip restores and improves roads previously destroyed by winter floods (torrentibus) Near Cuicul (Djemila). Source: ILS 5871. (244 AD)

name: Egypt
description: Nile flood normal. Location: flood normal 6-7 m nome Heracleopolis. Source: Bonneau 1971, 256. (250 AD)

name: Tiber
description: flooding. Location: senate created Gallienus Caesar, immediately Tiber floods. Source: Aurelius Victor De Caesaribus 32.4 (ed. Pichlmayr 108): statimq Tiberius adulta aestate diluvii faci. (253 AD)

name: Egypt
description: Nile flood late and normal?. Location: flood late and normal? 6-7 m nome Hermoupolis nome Oxyrhynchus. Source: Bonneau 1971, 257, 218 n6. (266 AD)

name: Egypt
description: Nile flood good. Location: flood good 7-8 m Memphis? nome Oxyrhynchus. Source: Bonneau 1971, 258. (291 AD)

name: Skirtos Edessa
description: flooding. Location: Edessa flooded, E. walls collapse, deaths, destruction in the whole plain of Edessa and Harran. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 4, citing Pseudo-Dionysius, Chronicon anonymum, ed. J.B. Chabot, CSCO 121 Scriptor. (303 AD)

name: Rhine
description: Cold: Rhine frozen. Location: Germanic coalition attracted across Rhine by frozen river, trapped on an island where the river divides, besieged by Roman vessels sent down to them; some captured by Romans, others escaped. Otherwise unknown.. Source: XII Panegyrici 6 (7).6.4, ed. Mynors 190.6-10.. (304 AD)

name: Tyre, Sidon
description: Earthquake. Location: Tyre, Sidon struck by earthquake. Source: Jerome, Chronicle, 304; Orosius, Seven Books Against the Pagans, 7.25. (304 AD)

name: Palestine, Egypt, Syria
description: drought. Location: winter rains did not come, causing famine, pestilence,  mass graves. Source: Eusebius, Historia ecclesiastica, 9, 8, 1; Michael Syrus 6,10 (1.204).  See Teleles 2004, 1.20, 21 f. (311 AD)

name: East
description: famine epidemic. Location: drought produces famine, carbuncle epidemic. Source: Eusebius, Historia ecclesiastica, 9, 8, 1; Michael Syrus 6,10 (1.204); Lactantius De mortibus persec. (311 AD)

name: Thebais Egypt
description: food shortage. Location: food shortage. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 7, citing Vita Pachomii, Paralipomena 21-2. 2.73-4, 3.110-11; Teleles 2004,. (320 AD)

name: Constantinople
description: food shortage. Location: S wind did not blow, ships from Egypt did not reach Constantinople. Source: Eunapius Vitae sophistarum, 6,2, 9-12 (20). (330 AD)

name: Syria Cilicia Antioch Cyprus
description: pestilence and famine. Location: famine in East, Constantine organized food relief, 36000 measures wheat at Antioch; many deaths in Syria and Cilicia; possibly man made (so G. Downey). Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 5, citing Jerome, Chronicle a. 333, 677-8, 336 AD; Theophanes, Chronographi. (333 AD)

name: West
description: Earthquake. Location: Italy and Dyrrachium shake in giant earthquake. Source: Jerome, Chronicle, 346. (346 AD)

name: France southern
description: flooding. Location: more intense rains than usual flood rivers, block movement; Constantius' military action delayed because supplies slow to come from Aquitaine on account of torrential and frequent spring rains, floods. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus 14.10.2; also Teleles 2004, 1.93-4, 20. (354 AD)

name: SE France en route to Rhine, Germany
description: snow. Location: Snow still on roads, even though army's departure to attack Alamannians had been delayed by spring floods in Aquitaine.. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus 14.10.1-6. 1.26.23-27.26 emensis itaque difficultatibus multis et niue obrutis . (354 AD)

name: Jerusalem
description: food shortage. Location: food shortage triggered sale of Jerusalem church's objects. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 10, citing Sozomen 4,25, 3 and Teleles 2004, no. 18; Possibly connected wit. (357 AD)

name: Meuse
description: freezing. Location: Julian used warships to keep ice from forming, which was unusual.. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus 17, 2, 2-3: Julian feared barbarian crossing on moonless night if Meuse froze; . (357 AD)

name: Although winters in Paris were usually quite mild, one was exceptionally cold, so that ice like slabs of marble floated in Seine; Julian was almost asphyxiated by carbon monoxide from braziers he had lighted to keep himself warm
description: winter much colder than usual at Paris. chunks of ice floating on the Seine. Source: Julian, Misopogon, 341B-C. (357 AD)

name: Aquitania
description: late summer?. Location: possible cold spring and summer. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus 17.8.1, 1.117.10-13:  nec enim egredi poterat, antequam ex Aquitania aestatis r. (358 AD)

name: Nicomedia
description: Earthquake; foul mist; wind. Location: Wind, whirlwind and waterspouts, foul mist, dark sky, as earthquake destroys Nicomedia. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus, 17.7.2-4. (358 AD)

name: Danube
description: flooding. Location: Julian uses pontoon bridge. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus 17.12.4. (358 AD)

name: Throughout the east
description: Sky darkened: atmospheric event. Location: Skies obscured by dark mist, stars visible through it, sun temporarily disappears. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus, 20.3.1. (360 AD)

name: Persian frontier
description: Continual rain. Location: Continual rains drench ground, stalling campaign. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus, 20.11.25. (360 AD)

name: Antioch
description: drought. Location: no rain (Dec-Feb?) 361-362. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus, Teleles 2004, no. 30; possibly also drought referred to Lebanon, in Vita Epiph. (361 AD)

name: East, Antioch
description: Drought. Location: Towards beginning of winter, scarcity of water to the extent that rivers and springs dry up. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus, 22.13.4; cf Sperber 1974, 281, nos. 11, 12, 13,  and above drought 361-2. (363 AD)

name: Euphrates
description: Flood. Location: On Julian's Persian Expedition, the river overflowed its banks and grain ships were sunk. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus, 24.1.11. (363 AD)

name: Sea rises and then recedes on 10 kalends of August; probably the same as 365 event
description: Sea overflows boundaries. Source: Consularia Italica an. 363, line 271; MGH Auct Ant 9 p. 294. (363 AD)

name: East Mediterranean
description: Sea recedes then floods; tsunami. Location: Sea recedes, revealing depths, then sweeps ashore and destroys cities; mare ultra terminos suos egressum est die 12 kal. Aug.. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus, 26.10.17; Consularia Constantinopolitana, an. 365; MGH Auct Ant 9 p. 240. (365 AD)

name: Châlons-sur-Marne
description: winter cold. Location: winter cold debilitated barbarian troops: unusual?. Source: Teleles 2004, citing Ammianus Marcellinus 27.2. (365 AD)

name: West, Sicily
description: Earthquake. Location: Earthquake causes tsunamis. Source: Jerome, Chronicle, 366. (366 AD)

name: Phrygia Cappadocia
description: drought. Location: famine causes refugees Constantinople, caused by lack of winter rains. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 43, citing Gregory Nyssa, Vita Macrinae, 12 (w/o date) well dated Teleles 2004, 1.. (368 AD)

name: Carthage
description: food shortage. Location: grain shortage covered by sales of wheat destined for R; replaced at profit by great harvest; 368 was last year of his proconsulate:  see PLRE. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 20, citing Ammianus Marcellinus 28, 1, 17-23 (2.63-4). (368 AD)

name: Danube, Thrace
description: Flood. Location: Valens prevented from operations across Danube because the river was flooded; some sense that it was exceptional in Ammianus and based on imperial plans. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus, 27.5.5. (368 AD)

name: Nicaea
description: Earthquake. Location: Nicaea ruined by earthquake. Source: Jerome, Chronicle, 368. (368 AD)

name: Cappadocia; Phrygia
description: Famine. Location: Basil preaches series of sermons inveighing the rich to share their grain; Great famine in Phrygia acc to Jerome and CC. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 21, citing Gregory of Nyssa, Vita Macrinae, 12 (w/o date) well dated Telele. (368 AD)

name: Dumnissum (Kirchberg, Rheinland-Pfalz), Germania I
description: drought. Location: Ausonius passes by the parched fields of Dumnissum: "praetereo arentem sitientibus undique terris/Dumnissum". Source: Ausonius, Mosella 6-7; McCormick in press. (371 AD)

name: Gaul Germany
description: Cold long winter. Location: autumn came on quickly, in a rush: autumno praecipito in Ammianus;. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus 30.14; Zosimus 4.17.1-2. (375 AD)

name: Rhine
description: Freezing Cold. Location: Lentienses Alamanni cross the frozen Rhine in February. Source: Ammianus Marcellinus, 31.10.4. (378 AD)

name: Illyricum
description: famine. Location: famine and epidemic; man made?. Source: Ambrose Expositio Evangelii secundum Lucam, 10, 10, CCL 14.148. (378 AD)

name: Rome?
description: Famine. Location: Next year will be famine; trouble with 'classis'. Source: Symmachus, Epistles, 2.6; MGH Auct Ant 6.1, p. 44. (383 AD)

name: Gaul, Pannonia, Rhaetia II, Liguria and Venetia
description: good harvest. Location: Ambrose, who also adds that not all provinces had bad harvests: Gaul, Pannonia, Rhaetia II, Liguria and Venetia had good harvest.. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 29, citing Ambrose, Ep. 10,73, 17-21. (383 AD)

name: Rome all provinces; Egypt;
description: drought poor harvest famine. Location: drought caused poor harvest in all provinces; famine at Rome according to Symm; Nile did not rise, according to Ambrose, who also adds that not all provinces had bad harvests: Gaul, Pannonia, Rhaetia II, Liguria and Venetia had good harvest.. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 29, citing Symmachus, Relatio 3, MGH Auct Ant 6.1, p. 283. (383 AD)

name: Italy Africa Sicily Sardinia
description: famine. Location: famine affects these provinces; same as famine in Rome 383-384?. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 29, citing Ambrosiaster, Quaestiones Veteris et Novi Testamenti, Quaestio 1. (383 AD)

name: Spain
description: winter floods ruin road. Location: previous winter floods. Source: L'Année épigraphique 1957, no. 311 and ibid., Année 1960, no. 158.. (383 AD)

name: Po valley
description: good harvest. Location: good harvest after bad. Source: Ambrose ep. 18,20-1 with Heide 1997, 174. (384 AD)

name: Antioch, Syria
description: excessive rainfall. Location: had bad impact on crops (famine already present?); pest infect city food ("mice" snakes locusts). Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 30, citing Libanius, Orat. 27, 3 3.24; mice: 50, 31 (3.484). (384 AD)

name: Thebaid
description: food shortage. Location: large numbers of locals came to monastery looking for food, miraculously provided. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 32, citing Historia monachorum in Aegypto, 7,44-47, Teleles 2004, no. 55.. (385 AD)

name: Rome Milan
description: poor grain harvest famine. Location: poor crops that year; no grain import (from Africa, pres); barren year: will not have rendered even seed sown. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 33, citing Symmachus Ep. 2, 52, Ambrose, De Ioseph, CSEL 32.2.38, p. 99. Cf. (388 AD)

name: Danube
description: freezing. Location: Because Danube frozen, barbarians captured Halmyris, near mouth of Danube.  Was this unusual?. Source: Philostorgius 10, 6 re episcopate of Eunomius. (388 AD)

name: Nile
description: unusual flow. Location: late rising, then heavier than usual flooding, threatening Alexandria. Source: Sozomen 7.20.4, Theophanes, AM  5884, 73.7. (392 AD)

name: Egypt lake Mareotis
description: great cold. Location: around Epiphany; unclear if unseasonal. Source: Teleles no. 56, citing Historia monachorum 26, 10. (394 AD)

name: Gaza
description: famine. Location: caused by drought; sounds like just starting when rain came.... Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 35, citing Mark the Deacon, Vita Porphyrii 19, 16.18-19. (395 AD)

name: Syria, Daskart
description: drought. Location: saint brings rain after drought. Source: Chronicle of Seert 1, 68, p. 210.. (397 AD)

name: Sardis
description: heavy rain. Location: intense rain caused rivers to be impassable and blocked Gainas from attacking Sardis. Source: Chronicle of Seert 1, 68, p. 210.. (399 AD)

name: ?Pannonia, Sea of Azov?
description: famine. Location: famine drove Vandals from Sea of Azov to Franks and Rhine; thus Procopius, Telelis says they were already in Pannonia by 395. Source: Chronicle of Seert 1, 68, p. 210.. (400 AD)

name: Rhone
description: freezing. Location: Rhone frozen across for people and horses between Dauphine & Vivarais. Source: Weikinn 1958, 1.6, cites a French meteorological treatise (M. Villard) but there does not appear to . (400 AD)

name: Asia Minor, Cilicia, Sisa
description: heavy snowfall. Location: sounds like highly local phenomenon? See Festugiere?. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 66, citing Theodoret Historia religiosa 26, 2. (400 AD)

name: Asia Minor, Ancyra
description: famine. Location: during famine, Verus and his wife give grain in many villages. Source: Hist. Lausiaca, 66. (400 AD)

name: Constantinople?
description: extreme winter. Location: much snow huge hail, 8 lbs, terrible cold; flooding. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 38, citing Teleles 2004, no. 68-1, Philostorgius 11, 7. (401 AD)

name: Black Sea
description: Black Sea Frozen. Location: Maris Pontici superficies ita gelu frenata est, ut per triginta dies soluta tandem glacies instar montium per Propontidem superne portata ducurreret.. Source: Weikinn 1958, p6,  Marcellinus Comes, 401.2; MGH Auct Ant 11 p. 67; Chronicon paschale, Bonn 1.568;. (401 AD)

name: Kukusos Cappadocia
description: famine. Location: Chrysostom suffered from famine in exile. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 41, citing Chrysostom Ep 2 ad Innocentium PG 52.535-5; Palladius, Dialogue . (405 AD)

name: Gaul; west according to Fredegar
description: Famine. Location: Great famine in Gaul; Famis dira grassatur.. Source: Gallic Chronicle of 452 line 72; MGH Auct Ant 9 p. 654; Fredegar, SS rer Merov 2, MGH p. 71. (411 AD)

name: Edessa, area, Harran
description: flood of Skirtos. Location: in middle of night floods city, buildings, wall collapse. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 76, citing Pseudo-Dionysius 142.14. (413 AD)

name: eastern empire? Italy; western empire?
description: famine caused by drought. Location: drought followed eclipse; after solar eclipse (stars appeared) terrible drought with mortality of men and beast. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 47; Teleles 2004, no. 77, citing Philostorgius 12, 8, p. 145; Constitutio S. (418 AD)

name: Black Sea coast
description: famine. Location: a famine in Pontos drives locals to castrate sons and sell as eunuchs. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 78, citing Cedrenus 1, 590.7 (from Ps Symeon?). (421 AD)

name: Constantinople? Elsewhere
description: earthquakes in many places, famine. Location: Terrae motus multis in locis fuit et frugum inedia subsecuta. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 81, citing Marcellinus Comes 76.4. (423 AD)

name: Asia Minor? ?Cilicia ?Syria
description: drought; famine; epidemic. Location: drought, crop failures, famine, and epidemic foretold 2 yrs in advance by saint. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 82, citing Theodoret, Historia religiosa 26, 19. (429 AD)

name: Hippo
description: famine. Location: famine among besieging Vandals; man-made?. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 53, citing Procopius, Bellum Vandalorum 1, 3, 34, 1.324. (431 AD)

name: Jerusalem Judaea
description: drought. Location: drought, reservoirs of monastery of St. Euthymius dry up, miraculous rains. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 85, citing Vita Euthymii 25; end on date 8 of Epiphany of uncertain year. (431 AD)

name: Cappadocia, Galatia
description: severe winter. Location: on road survive snow (poor visibility) and cold such as bishops of region claimed had never seen. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 87, citing Vita Melaniae c. 56. (437 AD)

name: Chalcedon Bithynia
description: food shortage 3 years. Location: last 3 years according to Vita Hypatii ?. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 71, citing Vita Hypatii 31, 3-8. (440 AD)

name: Constantinople (presumably?)
description: Snow. Location: Such a great snowfall that didn't melt for six months; men, animals die.. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 58, citing Teleles 2004, no. 88, Marcellinus Comes, an. 443; MGH Auct Ant 1. (443 AD)

name: Bithynia
description: Rain and flood. Location: Many cities and estates in Bithynia were ruined by continous rains and flooding of the rivers. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 89, citing Marcellinus Comes, an 444.3; MGH Auct Ant 11 p. 81. (444 AD)

name: Various places; Constantinople
description: Earthquake, famine, plague. Location: Earthquakes followed by hunger and aerum pestifer odor. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 62, citing Marcellinus Comes, an. 447.1; MGH Auct Ant 11 p. 82; Isaac of An. (447 AD)

name: Thrace
description: hail size of eyeballs. Location: knocks ripened grapes from vines, 5 months before quakes and Hun attack. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 91, citing Vita Hypatii, 52. (447 AD)

name: Amaseia
description: Location: a famine for which Seleukos miraculously fills some granary. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 170, citing Eustratius Vita Eutychii 17. (448 AD)

name: Spain
description: Earthquakes, signs in the sky. Location: In Galicia there were constant earthquakes, and signs in the sky such as blood. Source: Hydatius, an. 451, Burgess p. 100. (451 AD)

name: Palestine, Phrygia, Galatia, Cappadocia, Cilicia ?N Africa
description: drought; famine; epidemic. Location: drought in all these regions caused food shortage, mortality, miraculous raining of food like manna. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 64, citing Teleles 2004, no. 94, Evagrius 2,6. 53.21-54.4. (451 AD)

name: west?
description: Earthquakes; sky obscured. Location: signs prefigure war with Attila; earthquakes constant, moon obscured. Source: Isidore of Seville, History of the Goths, MGH Auct Ant 11 p. 278. (453 AD)

name: Phrygia
description: Locusts. Location: Swarms of locusts take over Phrygia. Source: Marcellinus Comes, an. 456.1; MGH Auct Ant 11 p. 86. (456 AD)

name: Ardeche Durance Rhone
description: freezing winter harsh. Location: Rhone frozen  across. Source: Weikinn 1958, 1.7 cites a French meteorological treatise (M Villard) but cannot confirm ancient sour. (460 AD)

name: Cyzicus
description: Earthquake. Location: Walls of Cyzicus fall in earthquake. Source: Marcellinus Comes, an. 460.1; MGH Auct Ant 11 p. 87. (460 AD)

name: Constantinople Egypt
description: Nile fails. Location: already food shortage in Constantinople (man-made?); Nile fails to rise, fear for next year. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 68, citing Teleles 2004, no. 98, Vita of Marcellus 26, Chronicon paschale 5. (463 AD)

name: Auvergne, near Clermont-Ferrand,
description: poor crop year, but more abundant, richer (uberior) wine harvest than expected. Location: The phrase annus sterilis here refers to a failed year of crops.. Source: Sidonius Ep. 2.14 ed. Loyen, 2.85: very poor crops that year (annus sterilis), but the wine harvest . (465 AD)

name: Ravenna
description: Earthquake. Location: Earthquake rattled city of Ravenna. Source: Annals of Ulster, an. 466; cf. Marcellinus Comes ad a. 467. (466 AD)

name: Syria Mesopotamia
description: famine. Location: great famine and many locusts. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 69, citing Teleles 2004, no. 101, Agapius 159. (466 AD)

name: Constantinople Bithynia, Nicomedia,  Sapanca Goel Tk
description: torrential rains. Location: rains last 43 days; mountains moved into sea, villages washed away. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 100, citing Evagrius 2, 14, stating he takes it from Priscus. (467 AD)

name: Iberian Peninsula?
description: very harsh year: winter, spring summer & fall. Location: Weather harsh, the seasons all mixed up, agricultural products confused. Source: Hydatius, a. 468, ed. Burgess p122, ed. Mommsen MGH Auct Ant 11.35; Fredegar, MGH SS rer Merov 2.77. (468 AD)

name: Burgundy
description: Famine in Burgundy. Location: Huge famine ravaged Burgundy. Ecdicius sent carts to collect 4000 starving people from neighboring towns (civitates) and feed them at his residence. Gregory identifies this famine with that in which Patiens, bishop of Lyons, dispatched food to a series of named towns, as mentioned by Sidonius, who explicitly identifies the Gothic invasion (dated 473), as the cause of that famine. The famines are possibly but not certainly identical.. Source: Gregory of Tours, Historiae 2.24, MGH SS rer Merov 1.1.69-70; Sidonius Apollinaris, Epistulae 6.12. (470 AD)

name: Syria
description: famine. Location: great famine in reign of Zeno. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 73, Teleles 2004, no. 103, citing Chronicle of Seert 2, 4, p14. (474 AD)

name: All of (north) Africa
description: severe drought; famine; mass deaths. Location: severe drought caused mass deaths, refugees come to Carthage; incredible famine devastates all of Africa, not a drop of rain, earth remained yellow, vines did not sprout leaves, olive trees remained bare, fruit trees barren.  Rivers dry.. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 74, citing Victor of Vita, Historia persecutionis Africanae provinciae 3, 5. (477 AD)

name: Rome and Italy
description: Famine. Location: Famine causes troops of Theoderic to turn to cannibalism; Gelasius delivers the city of Rome from a famine.. Source: Consularia Italica, a. 492; MGH Auct Ant 9.319. (492 AD)

name: Africa and Gaul
description: drought causes food shortage in Rome. Location: general drought in Africa and Gaul. Source: Stathakopoulos 2004, no. 77, citing Gelasius Adversus Andromachum,  15 & 23, CSEL 35.1.458; 461. (492 AD)

name: Pontus
description: Earthquake. Location: Great earthquake shook Pontus. Source: Annals of Tigernach, a. 498; Annals of Ulster, a. 498; Chronicon Scottorum, a. 495; actually occurre. (499 AD)

name: Euphrates, Mesopotamia
description: sky obscured, flood. Location: sky darkened; Euphrates overflowed that same day. Source: Teleles 2004, no. 108, citing John of Ephesus 2, p463. (499 AD)

name: Elousa inland from Gaza
description: drought. Location: Drought and sandstorms were destroying vines.. Source: Procopius of Gaza (ca. 465-ca. 528) Ep. 81, Epistola et declamationes ed A. Garzya and R. Loenertz (. (500 AD)
